next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The refrigerator gave up its inventory in little insults.

Half an onion wrapped in a wet paper towel. One egg with a crack running through the shell like a hair in glass. A jar of mustard with more yellow dried around the lid than left inside. Soy sauce. A bell pepper that had gone soft at the stem. No meat. No coffee. No beer. Cold air rolled over Jet's knuckles and smelled faintly of mildew and old scallions. He shut the door before the motor could waste any more power pretending there was something worth cooling.

The traffic ticket was waiting for him on the galley screen, red and patient.

MARS MUNICIPAL ENFORCEMENT
OUTSTANDING VIOLATION
8,000 WOOLONGS
STATUS: OVERDUE

He hit dismiss. The screen chimed and brought it right back.

The Bebop sounded hungover. Hull ticking as the morning cold eased out of the metal. Life support humming in the walls. Somewhere below, a loose vent grille rattled every few seconds with the stubborn regularity of something Jet had meant to fix three days ago and now resented on principle. The whole ship smelled like engine grease, stale air, and the soup he had made two nights ago from the last of the mushrooms and a hope that had not lasted past dinner.

They had all come back at different hours.

Jet knew because he had heard the airlock cycle three separate times after midnight and because nobody was where they usually were this morning. Spike was stretched on the common-room couch without taking off his jacket, one forearm across his eyes, one foot on the floor like he had sat down for a minute and lost the argument with g...
open_hooks_to_carry: Jet at a bar counting water stains on the ceiling — the question is not 'was I responsible' but 'did I choose not to see'
Spike on a rooftop smoking, thinking about Ayane and Julia, unable to retrofit compassion he did not feel
prev_tail_excerpt:
"I think you knew enough about the kind of place it was," he said. "Same as me."

Jet wanted to argue. He wanted specifics and qualifiers and the whole nice scaffolding of a defense. Instead he heard himself say, "Yeah."

Spike nodded once. Not forgiveness. Not agreement. Just acknowledgment of a fact ugly enough to stand on by itself.

Then he was gone too.

That left Jet with Ed.

He looked over. "Kid-"

Ed clicked the monitor dark.

"I'm not a kid," Ed said automatically. Then, after a beat that made it worse, "Mostly."

Jet almost smiled and could not get there. "Right."

Ed slid off the chair. Ein followed immediately, brushing the back of Jet's calf on the way past as if checking that the large damaged animal in the galley was still alive. At the hatch Ed stopped.

"It was already broken," Ed said, still not looking at him. "The biscuit just made crunchy noises."

Before Jet could answer, Ed and Ein went out.

The common room stayed lit by the low amber night lamps and the spill from the galley. On the table the photograph caught a strip of light across Ayane's cheek. The bent drawer slide lay beside the sink. The loose drawer still hung open an inch.

Jet crossed the room and shut the drawer with his hip.

Then he stood there listening as the ship settled around him and, one by one down the corridor, door seals caught where they had never seemed loud before.
state_deltas_required: The weight of being known — each crew member alone with the collapse of their protective anonymity
Each crew member privately reckons with exposure; physical separation mirrors emotional fracture; but the parallel structure shows them doing the same thing apart — already a family even in rupture
